metadata.title: An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking using computational text analysis tools

abstract: Government agencies struggle to use social media effectively to engage and involve the public. However, we know little about how individuals and groups on the other side of these efforts actually use social media to participate in government decision-making processes, independent of government-led engagement efforts. This exploratory study aims to address this by looking closer at how Twitter users selfmobilize after a particular event in which they may be motivated to participate. Specifically, the study examines Twitter activity before and after the United States Department of the Interior proposed changes to its Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) regulations that would limit public access to agency records. Acomputational text analysis of 5000 tweets using #FOIA over a 99-day period showed significant changes in the sentiment of the dialog before and after the proposed rule was announced. Furthermore, structural topic models show evidence of a diverse set of stakeholders using Twitter to exercise voice and share information about the proposed rule. Findings suggest that citizens and interest groups are taking to Twitter to mobilize participation in the rulemaking process prompting the need for more research on how the public uses social media to influence government decision-making, absent of government-led engagement efforts.

paragraphs: The growing literature on digitalization in public administration and policy has sought to identify important factors and conditions for governments' decisions to adopt social media, in addition to its many impacts. To date, this vein of research has focused overwhelmingly on the adoption and use of social media from the perspective of public sector organizations (Bertot, Jaeger & Hansen, 2012;Bertot, Jaeger & Grimes, 2012;Mergel, 2013;Mossberger et al., 2013;Zavattaro & Sementelli, 2014); mainly, how government agencies and municipalities leverage social media platforms in an effort to enhance democratic outcomes such as transparency and citizen participation (Crump, 2011;Gunawong, 2015;Hand & Ching, 2011;Reddick et al., 2017;Song & Lee, 2016).

In the case of social media-enabled participation, in particular, much of the literature assumes that participation occurs by way of government agencies and municipalities (hereafter referred to broadly as "governments") interacting directly with the public on social media. In other words, the assumption often is that fruitful interactions between citizens and government will occur almost automatically as a result of governments using social media. Yet, research also shows that social media-enabled public engagement efforts have been slow to realize their potential (Khan & Krishnan, 2017). Whether agencies are using their social media accounts to solicit feedback from citizens to be used in formal and informal decision-making processes (Sandoval-Almazan & Gil-Garcia, 2012), providing critical information during crises (Criado et al., 2020;Graham et al., 2015;Villodre & Criado, 2020), or sharing information about how to get involved in the community (Nabatchi & Mergel, 2010), governments typically exhibit a "build it and they will come" mindset. As a result, some public administration and policy scholars have been critical of the extent to which these government-led efforts have actually been effective in increasing meaningful public participation (Feeney & Porumbescu, 2020;Gunawong, 2015;Medaglia & Zheng, 2017).

Yet, research in fields adjacent to public administration has highlighted the ways social media attract attention to political issues, events, and specific social causes (Effing et al., 2011;Housholder & LaMarre, 2015;Shirky, 2011;Valenzuela, 2013;Vissers & Stolle, 2014;Warren et al., 2014). Indeed, research shows how Twitter and its use of the hashtag have been especially critical to promoting political and social movements (Chaudhry, 2014), driven largely by ad hoc publics formed around hashtags (Bruns & Burgess, 2011) created by users in response to current events, politics, and other topics of interest. This research has been key to understanding how individuals and groups are using Twitter to exercise voice, engage in advocacy (Saxton et al., 2015), and mobilize around important social and political issues (Bowles Eagle, 2015). However, an overwhelming majority of this work is in fields outside of public administration, likely due to the overwhelming focus on more political forms of participation such as elections, protests, and political activism. Nonetheless, it illustrates the ways in which the public regards social media as a venue to exercise voice, often sharing concerns and providing feedback that is useful not just to candidates and elected officials but also to government agencies and municipalities.

Meanwhile, governments typically struggle to generate that same level of engagement on their own social media accounts (Gunawong, 2015), resorting to using platforms as a means of pushing out information (Mergel, 2013), instead of exploiting its two-way functionality to engage with citizens in more meaningful ways. The result has been unsuccessful attempts to use social media to consult, involve, and even collaborate with citizens despite the affordances of social media applications (Medaglia & Zheng, 2017). However, the literature might only be telling half of the story. This is because, to date, the literature has focused primarily on government and less on users of social media (Medaglia & Zheng, 2017). This means that evaluations of social media as tools for citizen participation have been largely one-sided. Put another way, assessments of how effective social media is at increasing public participation in government decision-making processes are based almost exclusively on how effective (or ineffective) governments have been at directly engaging citizens in these processes using social media. As discussed, there is evolving research on social media and political participation which shows that citizens are indeed using social media platforms to self-mobilize around elections and alongside political and social protests. But there has been much less attention paid to how citizens might be engaged in similar forms of mobilization and collective action via social media in ways that directly affect public administrations. In the absence of efforts made by government agencies to formally engage the public, how might citizens be self-mobilizing on social media in ways that influence core public administration functions, including bureaucratic policy-making, regulatory rulemaking, and, even policy implementation?

This exploratory study takes a first stab at this question by looking specifically at how the public used Twitter to share information, exercise voice, and mobilize participation in the federal agency rulemaking process. Specifically, the study examines Twitter activity before and after the Department of the Interior (Interior) proposed changes to its Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) regulations in late 2018 (Federal Register, 2018). The proposed rule would grant the agency greater discretion in rejecting requests for records it deemed "too burdensome" (Federal Register, 2018) and, in doing so, significantly reduce agency transparency and increase administrative burdens on requesters (Kick, 2019).

In the end, the study analyzed 5000 tweets over a 99-day period using natural language processing (NLP) tools to explore how Twitter activity changed before and after the Interior's announced the rule. The computational text analysis indicates that there were notable changes in the sentiment and topics of the tweets following the Interior's announcement. A closer look at the tweets during the public comment period provides evidence of an active and diverse community of stakeholders who were leveraging Twitter to circulate information about the details of the proposed rule, including the significance of its impact on transparency and agency accountability if passed. Additionally, users were sharing information on how others could submit a public comment before the brief comment period closed. These findings suggest that Twitter helped not only to encourage stakeholders to participate in the rulemaking process but also further helped to widely distribute key information necessary for mobilizing even broader public participation in the process.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
paragraphs: Citizen participation is essential for democracy to function as it is intended. Participation is not just a democratic ideal though; it has been linked to many positive outcomes such as enhanced effectiveness and decision-making, and increased legitimacy and public trust (Barber, 2003;Bingham et al., 2005;Callahan, 2007;Fung, 2015;Irvin & Stansbury, 2004;Roberts, 2015). In practice, citizen participation is operationalized in many different ways and can refer to the activities of a wide range of stakeholders including citizens, industry, and civil society organizations such as nonprofits, nongovernmental organizations (NGOs), religious organizations, and interest groups (Yang & Callahan, 2005). More specifically, participation "is an umbrella term that describes the activities by which people's concerns, needs, interests, and values are incorporated into decisions and actions on public matters and issues" (Nabatchi & Leighninger, 2015, p. 6).

Participation takes place in a variety of forms such as participatory budgeting forums (Berner, 2001;Ebdon & Franklin, 2006;Franklin & Ebdon, 2015;Goldfrank, 2012;Sintomer et al., 2012;Wampler, 2012), public hearings (Adams, 2004;Cole & Caputo, 1984), citizen juries (Nabatchi & Munno, 2014), coproduction (Bovaird, 2007;Jo & Nabatchi, 2018;Nabatchi et al., 2017), deliberation (Hibbing & Theiss-Morse, 2002;Neblo et al., 2010;Schachter & Liu, 2005), and regulatory rulemaking (Coglianese, 2008;Rosener, 2015). It also encompasses community-led efforts independent from governments, including tree planting, community re-building, and other community-based activities (Nabatchi & Leighninger, 2015).

Scholars have outlined forms of participation according to the types of activities and democratic functions they fulfill. While there are several different frameworks, most are organized along a spectrum of passive to active forms of participation. For instance, some of the most passive forms of participation include presence, compliance, and even consultation; whereas, more active forms of participation include cooperation, coproduction, and collective action (Arnstein, 1969;Cornwall, 2006;Head, 2007). It is important to note that participation largely refers to the activities of citizens and communities, while engagement refers to ongoing, active efforts by government agencies and actors to involve the public. Engagement efforts are often aimed at eliciting the forms of participation discussed above (Head, 2007;Nabatchi & Leighninger, 2015).

The ubiquity of the internet has certainly shifted the ways in which the public participates and governments engage citizens. Indeed, the internet remains an essential venue for political participation (Friess et al., 2021;White, 1997) and public deliberation (Esau et al., 2021) while social media represents a new medium for voice and agenda-setting on political and social issues (Boynton & Richardson, 2016) in ways that did not exist previously. Regardless of the medium, opportunities for individuals and groups to exercise voice (Fox, 2007) in ways that can influence government decision-making processes are foundational to many forms of participation. In this way, voice is integral to democratic processes as well. Hirschman (1970) argued further that voice is also a powerful tool for citizens who are dissatisfied with specific actions or the general status quo. By exercising their voice, stakeholders can call public attention to their dissatisfaction, which often stems from instances of government failure. Individual-level voice can also influence group-level actions when leaders within communities use voice to inspire or mobilize their community to take action.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
paragraphs: Government agencies have their own definitions of participation in practice though. For instance, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) defines public participation as a process by which an agency directly engages members of the public in decision-making processes. In the United States, such processes typically include rulemaking which is the process that Executive and Independent agencies go through when developing and issuing policy (see Regulations.gov). This process of policy-making at the Federal agency level involves the agency first proposing a rule (also known as regulations) and issuing a notice of the proposed rule on the Federal Register, a centralized public system that has been publishing Executive orders and Presidential documents since 1936. This notice of proposed rulemaking then kicks off a public comment period.

The Administrative Procedure Act (1946) established this process of notice and comment in which agencies are required to inform the public of new or proposed amendments to procedures and rules and to fully consider the public's input in decisions to proceed with the rulemaking process (Rinfret, 2011;Rinfret & Furlong, 2012). Thus, rulemaking remains a well-defined process that seeks to provide transparency in the development of public policies, especially at the agency level, and to involve the public by providing an allotted amount of time to submit substantive input (Rinfret et al., 2022), including criticism of the proposed rule (Carlitz & Gunn, 2002). By law, the agency must consider all comments submitted and explain how the public's input informed the ultimate decision regarding the proposed rule, making public participation integral to the rulemaking process.

U.S. government agencies have increasingly been looking to ICTs to facilitate the rulemaking process, including launching web portals designated for soliciting public comment (Carlitz & Gunn, 2002;Farina et al., 2010;Lyons, 2018;Schlosberg et al., 2009). E-rulemaking, as it is called, allows for the electronic submission of public comments through Regulations.gov, an online portal. Public participation in rulemaking, more generally, has long been lauded for its ability to promote democratic participation (Lubbers, 2002) and the process of leveraging digital technology for e-rulemaking only further bolstered such claims (De Figueiredo, 2005;Johnson & Roman, 2015;Mendelson, 2011;Shulman, 2003). However, there has been substantial debate about the extent to which comments are representative of the public interest (Bingham et al., 2005). Even in the case of e-rulemaking, evidence suggests that the comments both received and considered by agencies are skewed in favor of the interests of business and industry (Golden, 1998;Yackee, 2006) with more recent research highlighting the potentially significant role that bots and fake comments play (Rinfret et al., 2022). This has provoked a dynamic discussion in the literature regarding not just e-rulemaking and public participation, but the democratic implications of digitalization, more generally.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
sections.header_raw: SOCIAL MEDIA AS A PUBLIC ENGAGEMENT TOOL
sections.header_norm: social media as a public engagement tool
paragraphs: The internet has not only paved the way for new methods of public participation in the rulemaking process. The pervasiveness of social media, for example, is believed to increase democratic participation by reaching audiences not historically involved in the political process (Bertot et al., 2010;Mergel, 2013) and providing citizens with new opportunities to engage in dialogue directly with public agencies and officials (Bertot, Jaeger & Hansen, 2012;Karakiza, 2015;Linders, 2012).

The extant literature assumes that one of the prevailing benefits of social media use in the public sector is its inherent bidirectional nature. The many-to-many capabilities of social networking sites allow governments and citizens to move beyond more traditional one-way participatory channels (Khan & Khan, Zavattaro & Sementelli, 2014) to bidirectional interactions that, when leveraged effectively, can open the door for participation and collaboration among government and its stakeholders (Mergel, 2013). However, the literature thus far has largely assumed that effective social media-enabled participation occurs almost exclusively in a bidirectional manner. For example, a government agency uses social media to interact with the public or solicit feedback using their Twitter or Facebook accounts (Bertot, Jaeger & Hansen, 2012;Kavanaugh et al., 2012). Members of the public then respond from their own personal accounts initiating an assumedly productive interaction between citizens and government. Yet, the literature has shown that governments' use of social media to engage with the public this way has largely failed to reach its potential as a tool for meaningful citizen engagement (Magro, 2012;Zavattaro & Sementelli, 2014). There are many proposed reasons for this failure, including an absence of strategy and clearly defined goals for social media use (Magro, 2012), lack of effort made by governments to apply the feedback acquired from citizens through social media interactions (Bertot et al., 2010), and an over-reliance on a "push" strategy (Mergel, 2013) that by and large neglects the two-way communication features afforded by social media platforms (Mossberger et al., 2013). This has led to doubt about whether social media is a viable tool for meaningful citizen participation after all; namely, whether it can actually impact policy-making, problem-solving, and other agency-level decision-making processes as once promised (Bertot, Jaeger & Hansen, 2012).

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
paragraphs: Despite claims of unmet potential, it would appear from media reports and the like that individuals and groups are indeed using social media for political participation purposes. Numerous sociopolitical movements around the world have started on social networking sites such as Twitter and have indeed resulted in high levels of civic engagement, participation, and collective action (Chaudhry, 2014;De Choudhury et al., 2016). Mobilization via digital media in this regard has been well-explored in the political science and political communication literatures in recent years. As a result, we have some understanding of how social media are mobilizing activism, or hashtag activism as it has become known (Xiong et al., 2019), around social issues and encouraging greater participation in political processes among individuals (Nekmat et al., 2015) and advocacy groups (Obar, 2014).

Thus far, there have been prominent examples of social media used as a platform and tool for mobilizing action against climate change (Segerberg & Bennett, 2011) and in more targeted demonstrations such as The Arab Spring (Moussa, 2017) and Australian Aboriginal activism (Petray, 2011). Most recently, online movements such as #WomensMarch, #DefundThePolice, and #BlackLivesMatter have translated to offline protests, the latter of which is now considered the largest civil rights demonstration in history following the unjust murders of George Floyd, Breonna Taylor, and Ahmaud Arbery in the early months of 2020. These are just a few examples of citizens taking to social media to engage politically, in both conventional and unconventional ways. Whatever the larger cause might be, citizens are taking to social media to share information and encourage other individuals to get involved in democratic activities by signing e-petitions and form letters, and tweeting at agencies and public officials to demand action and change. Online participation has not in any way replaced more traditional forms of offline participation such as protesting, letter writing, phone banking, and voting. Yet, using social media to mobilize individuals to participate in both online and offline activities appears to be prevalent. While the forms of engagement evident in these cases may not have been driven intentionally by public sector agencies as was expected, the feedback provided by citizens via social media and the types of offline actions encouraged, more or less achieve many of the same democratic goals; most notably, citizen participation and voice.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
sections.header_raw: Can social media-enabled participation influence government decision-making?
sections.header_norm: can social media enabled participation influence government decision making
paragraphs: In all, most of the government social media literature evaluates the effectiveness of social media on the basis of its ability to facilitate citizen consultation and largely ignores how more active forms of participation such as collective action can also impact government decisionmaking processes. Yet, in the social and political movement cases discussed above, citizen participation is not activated by government agencies pushing or pulling, or through consultation between governments and citizens. Instead, individuals and groups not associated with government agencies appear to be the ones "pushing" information out to other members of the public and, at times, even directly to governments and public officials. In short, active participation is not occurring as a result of an orchestrated bidirectional interaction between citizens and government agencies, as often assumed.

The public's use of social media to participate in government decision-making activities beyond the assumed bidirectional interaction is an underexplored area of research in public administration, especially in the digital government literature. Given that there are mixed results on whether social media-enabled participation has indeed achieved its potential, it seems pertinent to examine other ways in which the public's use of social media influences government activities and decisions. For instance, given relevant findings on social media-led socio-political mobilization in the political science and sociology literature (Margetts et al., 2015;Obar, 2014;Segerberg & Bennett, 2011), it is important to ask how citizens are leveraging social media to self-mobilize in ways that affect policy-making and government decision-making processes. Indeed, this paper aims to address this limited perspective by looking at how individuals and groups used social media to share information, exercise voice, and mobilize participation in an effort to influence a federal agency's rulemaking process.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
sections.container_raw: AN EXPLORATORY ANALYSIS OF SOCIAL MEDIA AND MOBILIZATION BEHAVIOR
sections.container_norm: an exploratory analysis of social media and mobilization behavior
paragraphs: In late 2018, the United States Department of the Interior (Interior) quietly announced a proposed rule to its FOIA regulations. To better understand the context of this event, it is important to first provide some necessary background information. First, the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) was adopted in 1966 as a means of promoting transparency in government and helping citizens exercise their First Amendment rights. FOIA establishes that government agencies must make specific kinds of information deemed in the public interest publicly available, and that agencies must supply any additional information requested, upon such a request. Leading up to the time of the Interior's notice of proposed rulemaking, the Interior was experiencing an influx in information requests. Over the course of the fall of 2018, former Interior Secretary, Ryan Zinke 1 , was part of more than a dozen federal investigations related to ethics scandals (Friedman, 2018; Office of the Inspector General, 2022). This prompted a surge in FOIA requests and raised questions regarding the transparency and internal activities of the agency.

The proposed rule granted bureaucrats at the Interior greater discretion to reject requests that they deemed "unduly burdensome" or "unreasonable" with little to no guidance on what would qualify as such (Federal Register, 2018). Consequently, many journalists, advocacy groups, lawyers, and citizens were outraged as they claimed that the changes would make it even easier for the Interior to deny and delay requests for public records (Grand Canyon Trust, 2019;Kick, 2019; Open the Government, 2019; Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, 2019). In addition to access to information being a right awarded by FOIA and the First Amendment, journalists and lawyers were especially vocal about changes they believed would make it even more difficult for them to obtain information they relied on to protect the public interest.

The proposed rule was announced via The Federal Register website on Friday, December 28, 2018, just 3 days after the Christmas holiday and only a few days before the new year. The announcement also came about a week into the longest government shutdown in United States history (Zaveri et al., 2019). The timing prompted many to believe that the agency was trying to conceal the proposed rule from the public and, in doing so, stifle public input. The short public comment period of 30 days only exacerbated these claims. Advocacy groups immediately petitioned to extend the comment period to 120 days citing an insufficient amount of time for public comment and the uncertain impact of the government shutdown on federal agencies, including the Interior. Ultimately, the Interior extended the deadline by only one day, blaming a technical glitch for the need to extend the deadline to ensure the public had the full 30 days to comment. In all, 65,303 comments were submitted to Regulations.gov between December 28, 2018, and January 29, 2019.

In response to not just the stipulations of the proposed rule, but also the ongoing controversy around Zinke, the Interior, and what some interpreted as attempts to conceal the proposed rule and curb public involvement, the public may have been inclined to take to social media to exercise voice. Further the FOIA community could leverage social media (Schwoerer, 2019) to mobilize efforts to hold the agency accountable and encourage others to participate in the regulatory process. sections.text:
In late 2018, the United States Department of the Interior (Interior) quietly announced a proposed rule to its FOIA regulations. To better understand the context of this event, it is important to first provide some necessary background information. First, the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) was adopted in 1966 as a means of promoting transparency in government and helping citizens exercise their First Amendment rights. FOIA establishes that government agencies must make specific kinds of information deemed in the public interest publicly available, and that agencies must supply any additional information requested, upon such a request. Leading up to the time of the Interior's notice of proposed rulemaking, the Interior was experiencing an influx in information requests. Over the course of the fall of 2018, former Interior Secretary, Ryan Zinke 1 , was part of more than a dozen federal investigations related to ethics scandals (Friedman, 2018; Office of the Inspector General, 2022). This prompted a surge in FOIA requests and raised questions regarding the transparency and internal activities of the agency.

The proposed rule granted bureaucrats at the Interior greater discretion to reject requests that they deemed "unduly burdensome" or "unreasonable" with little to no guidance on what would qualify as such (Federal Register, 2018). Consequently, many journalists, advocacy groups, lawyers, and citizens were outraged as they claimed that the changes would make it even easier for the Interior to deny and delay requests for public records (Grand Canyon Trust, 2019;Kick, 2019; Open the Government, 2019; Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, 2019). In addition to access to information being a right awarded by FOIA and the First Amendment, journalists and lawyers were especially vocal about changes they believed would make it even more difficult for them to obtain information they relied on to protect the public interest.

The proposed rule was announced via The Federal Register website on Friday, December 28, 2018, just 3 days after the Christmas holiday and only a few days before the new year. The announcement also came about a week into the longest government shutdown in United States history (Zaveri et al., 2019). The timing prompted many to believe that the agency was trying to conceal the proposed rule from the public and, in doing so, stifle public input. The short public comment period of 30 days only exacerbated these claims. Advocacy groups immediately petitioned to extend the comment period to 120 days citing an insufficient amount of time for public comment and the uncertain impact of the government shutdown on federal agencies, including the Interior. Ultimately, the Interior extended the deadline by only one day, blaming a technical glitch for the need to extend the deadline to ensure the public had the full 30 days to comment. In all, 65,303 comments were submitted to Regulations.gov between December 28, 2018, and January 29, 2019.

In response to not just the stipulations of the proposed rule, but also the ongoing controversy around Zinke, the Interior, and what some interpreted as attempts to conceal the proposed rule and curb public involvement, the public may have been inclined to take to social media to exercise voice. Further the FOIA community could leverage social media (Schwoerer, 2019) to mobilize efforts to hold the agency accountable and encourage others to participate in the regulatory process. sections.container_raw: AN EXPLORATORY ANALYSIS OF SOCIAL MEDIA AND MOBILIZATION BEHAVIOR
sections.container_norm: an exploratory analysis of social media and mobilization behavior
paragraphs: The data used in the analysis is from the popular social media platform Twitter. The "advanced search" tool on Twitter was used to filter the tweets according to one specific hashtag (#FOIA) beginning November 1, 2018 through December 28, 2019, exactly 1 year after the rule had been announced. This hashtag was chosen based on its relevance to the Interior's proposed rule as well as a broader community of journalists, lawyers, advocacy organizations, and citizens who have an interest in FOIA (Yaremko & Walby, 2021) and actively use Twitter to share FOIA-related news with the community (Schwoerer, 2019). The python programming language was used to scrape the tweets using the GetOldTweets3 Python package.

It is important to note that the tweets analyzed were all original tweets posted by accounts during the sampling timeframe and do not include retweets. In other words, the data set does not include every retweet as a separate observation. Instead, the number of retweets of each original tweet is its own data point for each observation (i.e., original tweet). The sample of tweets used in this exploratory study (N = 5000) focused specifically on the time surrounding the proposed rule announcement and the public comment period that immediately followed. The proposed rule was announced on December 28, 2018 and the public comment period ended on January 29, 2019 which accounted for 33 full days in total. To compare the tweets before and after to those during the public comment period, I created three groups with 33 full days of tweets each resulting in three sub-samples of tweets: "precomment," "public comment," and "post-comment" period. The sub-sample of tweets labeled "pre-comment" contained all #FOIA tweets from November 25, 2018, to December 27, 2018. The "public comment" sub-sample of tweets contained all #FOIA tweets posted during the public comment period (December 28, 2018 to January 29, 2019). The "post-comment" subsample contained all #FOIA tweets from January 30, 2019, to March 3, 2019.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
sections.container_raw: AN EXPLORATORY ANALYSIS OF SOCIAL MEDIA AND MOBILIZATION BEHAVIOR
sections.container_norm: an exploratory analysis of social media and mobilization behavior
paragraphs: The first step in exploring the tweets was a descriptive comparison of the tweets before, during, and after the notice of the Interior's proposed rule on December 28, 2018, to identify changes in activity in the volume of #FOIA tweets across the three time periods. Second, a sentiment analysis of the tweets was conducted to analyze and identify changes in the relative tone and emotional affect of the Twitter dialog across the three periods. Finally, a method of text analysis called structural topic modeling (STM) was used to analyze the contents of the tweets to understand changes in the topics present in the Twitter dialog across time. The decision to use computational tools such as sentiment analysis and topic modeling was ultimately driven by their ability to analyze large batches of text to identify changes in affect (emotion) as well as latent themes which provided insight into the research question of interest in this study. Since there were 5000 individual tweets in the sample, hand-coding each tweet would have required a great deal of time and resources that were, unfortunately, out of reach. Whereas computational methods that leverage natural language processing (NLP) tools such as these can provide the additional technical capacity needed to analyze a large text-based data set (Anastasopoulos et al., 2017;DiMaggio, 2015). They are discussed in further detail below.

Sentiment analysis is a form of opinion mining (Hand & Ching, 2019;Zavattaro et al., 2015). It is a fairly popular methodological tool for analyzing social media data, especially. It relies on machine learning algorithms to interpret emotions and attitudes contained in batches of text and label them accordingly. In this study, The NRC Lexicon (Saif, 2021) sentiment analysis package in R was used which classifies the words and word groupings identified according to basic emotions including anticipation, anger, fear, disgust, joy, sadness, surprise, and trust. The algorithm also produces a sentiment score that ranges from 0 (no emotion identified) to 1 (emotion identified) and then calculates the number of times the emotion is identified in one document. It is important to note that tweets can be classified as multiple emotions. For instance, if "anger" is identified once in a document, it will have an anger sentiment score of 1 and, similarly, if the algorithm identifies two occurrences of "fear" in a document, it will generate a fear sentiment score of 2. The NRC emotion lexicon package classifies text as positive (+1.0) or negative (-1.0) with an additional total score based on the number of times a "positive" or "negative" word is identified in the document. Documents that are neither positive nor negative are considered "neutral" and receive a score of 0 for both.

Another type of NLP tool for analyzing large batches of text is the topic model. Topic modeling relies on machine learning algorithms to identify latent themes across a collection of documents (Anastasopoulos et al., 2017). Trained models parse the text and group words together in clusters based on themes and patterns detected, and by how frequently words are used, and the words they are most often associated with. The clusters are referred to as topics which the researcher(s) must then analyze qualitatively to infer an underlying theme (i.e., topic) among the word groupings. This process is not unlike conducting a thematic content analysis, only the machine learning algorithm allows for an automated content analysis relatively quickly from which the researcher(s) can interpret based on their domain expertise and the broader context from which the text comes from, and in ways that still hold up when assessing content, external, and predictive validity (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2017;Pandey et al., 2017).

There are two types of algorithms used most often in research of this kind: supervised and unsupervised. In unsupervised models, the algorithm relies on a generic dictionary to interpret the text (also referred to as documents) and generate clusters based on similarities and patterns it identifies. Whereas, supervised models are concerned with not only identifying patterns in the data but also predicting a documents' association with a predefined label or classification. Structural topic modeling (STM), the specific tool used in this study, is an unsupervised learning model popular for analyzing textual data (Roberts et al., 2014). STM is useful because it first identifies latent topics across a set of documents and then uses document-level covariates to identify the prevalence of the identified topic(s) across notable covariates (Bagozzi & Berliner, 2018). As such, this model allows for the time period the tweet comes from to be accounted for in the prior distributions for the topic proportions and topic-word distributions.

To date, a handful of studies in public administration and management research have used similar forms of text analysis to understand how local governments and public agencies communicate with stakeholders (Criado & Villodre, 2021;Hand & Ching, 2019;Zavattaro et al., 2015), including in the case of emergencies (Villodre & Criado, 2020;Kavanaugh et al., 2012), analyze public comments (Ingrams, 2020), and understand citizen attitudes in real-time (Kwon et al., 2016). Scholars have also demonstrated the promise of NLP techniques for understanding important functions of public organizations (Anastasopoulos et al., 2020) and proposing new measures in public administration (Pandey et al., 2017). This has led to calls for greater use of these tools to advance understanding of public administration theory and practice in new ways (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2017).

sections.container_raw: AN EXPLORATORY ANALYSIS OF SOCIAL MEDIA AND MOBILIZATION BEHAVIOR
sections.container_norm: an exploratory analysis of social media and mobilization behavior
paragraphs: Since this is an exploratory study, the aim of the analysis was to first identify patterns within each grouping of tweets by time period (pre-comment period, public comment period, and post-comment period) using sentiment analysis and STM and then note differences between and across the three periods. The initial descriptive analysis showed that the number of tweets (N = 1614) increased modestly by approximately 9.2% (149 more tweets) after the notice of the Interior's proposed rule was published on the Federal Register (N = 1763). Following the end of the public comment period, the amount of tweets returned to roughly the same as before the rule was announced (N = 1623). Table 1 illustrates the number of total tweets in each sub-sample.

paragraphs: To understand what is driving the changes in sentiment, the STM analyzed the contents of the dialog to identify the main topics present. The model first identified the top 10 words that make up each topic identified according to the FREX labeling metric (Bischof & Airoldi, 2012) which identifies the top words based on the frequency of the word and its exclusivity to that particular grouping of words, or topic (see Appendix for Model Fit Statistics & Diagnostics). The share of tweets that are related to that topic is represented by the "topic proportion."

Figure 1a shows the top topics of all tweets before the proposed rule was announced. As seen in the graph in Figure 1a, the top topic was Topic 3, accounting for approximately 20% of the tweets. Topic 3 appears to indicate tweets related to a lawsuit of interest within the FOIA community. Looking closer at a tweet identified by the algorithm as "highly associated" with the topic (see Table 2), it was clear that this dialog was in response to a lawsuit regarding states' attempts to exempt judicial records from public records. Topics 2 and 4 highlight the work of journalists conducting investigative work made possible by FOIA requests. Whereas, Topics 1 and 5 discussed two specific investigations that appear to have been made possible by those journalistic efforts.

Figure 1b illustrates the shift following the notice of the proposed rule and subsequent opening of the public comment period. The topic clusters and the "highly associated documents" (Table 2) indicate that the Interior's proposed rule dominated the discussion around #FOIA. Topics 2, 3, and 5 made up a total of 62.5% of the tweets from December 28, 2018, to January 29, 2019. These tweets discussed the implications of the rule as well as information and further instructions about how individuals could get involved by submitting a public comment on Regulations.gov.

Figure 1c illustrates the shift in dialog again following the close of the public comment period. Because of the wide diversity of the dialog during this time, the algorithm identified seven topics. The dialog appeared to return to similar topics as before the public comment period opened. For example, Topics 3, 4, and 7 were indicative of new updates in FOIArelated news. This included tips on how to share information about successful FOIA appeals (Topic 3) and efforts by MuckRock, a nonprofit journalism collaborative that advocates for open government and transparency, to support journalists (Topic 4). Similarly, the Virginia Coalition of Open Government (VCOG), another open government advocacy group, was active in sharing general news related to transparency and open government (Topic 7).

Finally, the STM was conducted for the full sample of tweets (N = 5000) across the full range of time (November 25, 2018to March 3, 2019). The top topics across the full range of time are quite general but are also clearly (and unsurprisingly) representative of the three individual sub-samples. When comparing the top topics from each time period individually to the top topics that emerged for the full 99-day period analyzed, we see that the top topics for the pre-public comment period and post-comment period are not only similar to one another, but are also heavily represented in the proportion of top topics within the full sample of tweets (67.3%). Topics related to the controversy surrounding the proposed rule and how to take action dominated the Twitter dialog during the public comment period (89.1%), but represented a little less than 33% of the full sample of tweets. This is discussed in more detail in the next section.

The dialog appeared to return to similar topics as before the public comment period opened. For example, Topics 3, 4, and 7 were indicative of new updates in FOIArelated news. This included tips on how to share information about successful FOIA appeals (Topic 3) and efforts by MuckRock, a nonprofit journalism collaborative that advocates for open government and transparency, to support journalists (Topic 4). Similarly, the Virginia Coalition of Open Government (VCOG), another open government advocacy group, was active in sharing general news related to transparency and open government (Topic 7).

Finally, the STM was conducted for the full sample of tweets (N = 5000) across the full range of time (November 25, 2018to March 3, 2019). The top topics across the full range of time are quite general but are also clearly (and unsurprisingly) representative of the three individual sub-samples. When comparing the top topics from each time period individually to the top topics that emerged for the full 99-day period analyzed, we see that the top topics for the pre-public comment period and post-comment period are not only similar to one another, but are also heavily represented in the proportion of top topics within the full sample of tweets (67.3%). Topics related to the controversy surrounding the proposed rule and how to take action dominated the Twitter dialog during the public comment period (89.1%), but represented a little less than 33% of the full sample of tweets. This is discussed in more detail in the next section.
sections.preview_head: To understand what is driving the changes in sentiment, the STM analyzed the contents of the dialog to identify the main topics present. The model first identified the top 10 words that make up each topic identified according to the FREX labeling metric (Bischof & Airoldi, 2012) which identifies the top words based on the frequency of the word and its exclusivity to that particular grouping of words, or topic (see Appendix for Model Fit Statistics & Diagnostics). The share of tweets that are related to that topic is represented by the "topic proportion."

Perhaps unsurprisingly considering the public backlash at the time (American Oversight, 2020; Bublé, 2022), the results of the sentiment analysis showed that the emotional sentiment of the tweets leaned more heavily toward "disgust" and "surprise" compared to before and after the public comment period. This was likely indicative of not just the outrage at the Interior's proposed rule and its implications, but also shock that the agency, which had already been under scrutiny.

Granted, this shift also makes the increase in positive sentiment especially interesting. A closer look at the topics that emerged during the public comment period suggests a likely explanation though. Topics 2-5 all indicate a high degree of information-sharing regarding the rule and public comment process. Due to the government shutdown at the time, there was a lack of information coming from the Interior (and the rest of the federal government), so it was largely up to citizens and interest groups to keep themselves and each other informed about what was going on, including what actions to take. The spirit of these tweets was especially optimistic about the public's ability to take action together and hold the agency accountable. Positive affirmations that the community could actually make a difference dominated the #FOIA dialog, as did assertions that the public had the "power to fight for their right to know." This implies that there was not just a spirit of mobilization among users but a sense of responsibility to exercise their democratic right to participate. For example, many in the community framed the issue as a threat to democracy and urged others to "let [their] voice be heard" by submitting a comment to Regulations.gov. Regardless of whether a user actually submitted a comment or not, Twitter users were rallying around #FOIA to demand greater transparency and accountability from their government.

The unexpected increase in trust sentiment during the comment period was likely indicative of the high degree of information sharing and efforts among advocacy groups to encourage public participation. Reputable advocacy organizations including MuckRock, the Sierra Club, and the Virginia Coalition for Open Government were active during the comment period lending credibility to information and using their platforms to encourage people to participate in the rulemaking process. It is not unlike interest groups to leverage mass campaigns to mobilize participation in the rulemaking process for issues they care

Likewise, active members and leaders within the FOIA community as well as journalists and media outlets were also actively using Twitter to spread the word to others outside of the immediate #FOIA community; an example of the power of hashtags and ad hoc publics on Twitter (Bruns & Burgess, 2011). Finally, the STM for the full 99-day period showed that the top topics for the pre-public comment period and post-comment period were quite similar to one another and represented 67.3% of the tweets in the full sample. On the other hand, the Interior and public comment periodrelated tweets represented a little less than ⅓ of the total tweets, despite making up almost 90% of all tweets during the public comment period. These findings suggest stability in the dialog and sense of community around #FOIA on Twitter. However, once the news of the Interior's proposed rule broke, this dialog shifted and the rule, including its potential impacts and how the public could take action, dominated. It stayed that way throughout the entirety of the designated public comment period. Once the comment period ended and the need to mobilize participation in the rulemaking process was no more, the dialog returned to its normal both in terms of the topics and the relative volume of tweets.

Perhaps unsurprisingly considering the public backlash at the time (American Oversight, 2020; Bublé, 2022), the results of the sentiment analysis showed that the emotional sentiment of the tweets leaned more heavily toward "disgust" and "surprise" compared to before and after the public comment period. This was likely indicative of not just the outrage at the Interior's proposed rule and its implications, but also shock that the agency, which had already been under scrutiny.

Granted, this shift also makes the increase in positive sentiment especially interesting. A closer look at the topics that emerged during the public comment period suggests a likely explanation though. Topics 2-5 all indicate a high degree of information-sharing regarding the rule and public comment process. Due to the government shutdown at the time, there was a lack of information coming from the Interior (and the rest of the federal government), so it was largely up to citizens and interest groups to keep themselves and each other informed about what was going on, including what actions to take. The spirit of these tweets was especially optimistic about the public's ability to take action together and hold the agency accountable. Positive affirmations that the community could actually make a difference dominated the #FOIA dialog, as did assertions that the public had the "power to fight for their right to know." This implies that there was not just a spirit of mobilization among users but a sense of responsibility to exercise their democratic right to participate. For example, many in the community framed the issue as a threat to democracy and urged others to "let [their] voice be heard" by submitting a comment to Regulations.gov. Regardless of whether a user actually submitted a comment or not, Twitter users were rallying around #FOIA to demand greater transparency and accountability from their government.

The unexpected increase in trust sentiment during the comment period was likely indicative of the high degree of information sharing and efforts among advocacy groups to encourage public participation. Reputable advocacy organizations including MuckRock, the Sierra Club, and the Virginia Coalition for Open Government were active during the comment period lending credibility to information and using their platforms to encourage people to participate in the rulemaking process. It is not unlike interest groups to leverage mass campaigns to mobilize participation in the rulemaking process for issues they care

about (Balla et al., 2019(Balla et al., , 2022;;Rinfret et al., 2022), but it was interesting to see the extent to which these groups were leveraging Twitter to do so. Likewise, active members and leaders within the FOIA community as well as journalists and media outlets were also actively using Twitter to spread the word to others outside of the immediate #FOIA community; an example of the power of hashtags and ad hoc publics on Twitter (Bruns & Burgess, 2011). Finally, the STM for the full 99-day period showed that the top topics for the pre-public comment period and post-comment period were quite similar to one another and represented 67.3% of the tweets in the full sample. On the other hand, the Interior and public comment periodrelated tweets represented a little less than ⅓ of the total tweets, despite making up almost 90% of all tweets during the public comment period. These findings suggest stability in the dialog and sense of community around #FOIA on Twitter. However, once the news of the Interior's proposed rule broke, this dialog shifted and the rule, including its potential impacts and how the public could take action, dominated. It stayed that way throughout the entirety of the designated public comment period. Once the comment period ended and the need to mobilize participation in the rulemaking process was no more, the dialog returned to its normal both in terms of the topics and the relative volume of tweets.

However, it is the results of the sentiment analysis and STM that provide the most insight into the ways that individuals and groups were taking to Twitter to exercise voice, share information, and mobilize participation, absent efforts by government agencies to involve the public.

sections.paper_id: Schwoerer - 2023 - An exploratory study of social media's role in facilitating public participation in e‐rulemaking usi
paragraphs: The goal of this study was to explore how individuals and groups use social media to participate in government decision-making processes, absent efforts made by a governmental body to directly engage the public via social media. To do this, I used computational text analysis to analyze 5000 tweets over a 99-day period surrounding the Department of the Interior's announcement of a controversial rule in 2018. The proposed rule would change the way the agency handled public information requests, limiting access to information and imposing new administrative burdens on requestors. My analysis showed that individuals and groups activitely used Twitter to share information and encourage members of the public to participate in the rulemaking process by submitting a comment to Regulations.gov.

This self-led mobilization was evident by a modest increase in the frequency of tweets containing the hashtag #FOIA during the allotted public comment period but, more importantly, by significant differences in the emotional sentiment of the dialog before, during, and after the comment period. Using STM, further analysis of the content of the tweets before, during, and after the public comment period showed that the predominant topics of conversation on Twitter surrounding #FOIA shifted between and across the three periods. Before the announcement of the rule, #FOIA was used to curate a general discussion about transparency and share FOIA-related information among a small but active community of FOIA advocates on Twitter. However, following the notice of the proposed rule and subsequent public comment period, the community surrounding #FOIA grew to include a much wider audience and the dialog was dominated by the proposed rule, its implications, and instructions on how individuals and groups could exercise voice and hold the agency accountable by submitting a public comment in objection to it. Once the public comment period had concluded, dialog returned to essentially the same topics and similar sentiments as before the rule was announced.

Ultimately, in October 2019, the Interior announced that it would not implement the rule, after all, leaving to question the role that mobilization via Twitter played in not only encouraging public participation in the e-rulemaking process but also the agency's decisionmaking process. While such an analysis was beyond the scope of this paper, future research should consider looking closer at the public comments that were submitted to investigate whether Twitter could have indeed been a driver of traffic to Regulations.gov during this time. Furthermore, Topic 5 during the public comment period was indicative of a high volume of nearly identical tweets posted by hundreds of different users tagging @SierraRise (affiliated with the Sierra Club). The tweets linked to Regulations.gov where users could submit a comment. A closer look at these tweets and others posted around the same time shows evidence that Twitter was being used by non-profits and advocacy groups as a channel for distributing templates and e-form letters that had been adapted for Twitter. These templates were specifically intended for members of the public to use when submitting their own comments. This practice of using digital media to conduct mass comment campaigns in administrative rulemaking is only beginning to be explored by scholars (Balla et al., 2019(Balla et al., , 2022;;Rinfret et al., 2022). The emerging research emphasizes the magnitude of these mass comment campaigns and their influence on the rulemaking process. However, to my knowledge, there has been very little research to date that has explored the role of social media platforms such as Twitter in organizing such mass comment campaigns. Therefore, a closer look at how nonprofits and advocacy groups, specifically, are using social media to coordinate and execute mass comment campaigns is an important area of future research. Additionally, exploring further the role of social media, as well as interest and advocacy groups' use of new and emerging social media platforms (Wiley et al., 2022), in mediating public participation processes remains an area rich for further research as it speaks not only to the multitude of actors that are involved in enabling democratic participation but also in holding government accountable.

This research is, of course, not without its limitations. First, this is just one snapshot in time of social media dialog around one specific topic, on one platform, and in one policy domain. Future research should look at other cases of rules or policies in order to observe whether similar patterns and changes in social media behavior are present. Furthermore, the computational text analysis tools, especially the STM, are helpful for conducting an exploratory analysis such as this but they also have limitations. Although this analysis did incorporate an ANOVA to look at whether the observed variance in sentiment across the time periods was statistically significant, this method of analysis is not suitable for probing causal mechanisms. In other words, this study is intended to provide descriptive data of observed changes in observed behavior during a specific time period and is not intended to make causal claims about any potential links between the notice of the rule, the changes in user behavior observed, and the agency's final decision. Similarly, there were approximately 65,000 public comments submitted to Regulations.gov during the public comment period, but only 9644 were available for download on the website. Therefore, analyzing the Twitter activity alongside the public comment submissions to try to understand the potential relationship between the two becomes quite difficult. As such, while extremely useful as discussed previously, further analysis of the potential relationship between the tweets and public comments exceeds the scope of this particular study and remains an exciting area of future research. 2 Nonetheless, these results suggest that individuals and groups are indeed using social media platforms such as Twitter to self-organize and mobilize participation in government decision-making processes, much like they do in more political contexts such as elections. In this case, however, the aim was not to encourage fellow voters but to encourage public participation in a federal agency's rulemaking process. The information shared among the FOIA community on Twitter was especially valuable at the time given the lack of response from the agency due to the federal government shutdown. Therefore, Twitter appeared to play an important role not just for coalition building but also for improving transparency and strengthening channels for accountability.

These are forms of participation that are crucial to democracy, yet they are not necessarily captured if we view social media-enabled participation strictly from a bidirectional point of view. In other words, if we only think about social media-enabled participation in terms of governments' use of social media to engage directly with citizens and, conversely, citizens' use of social media to engage directly with governments, then we overlook other, perhaps more indirect, ways that the public uses social media to participate. In light of previous research on the limits of social media, this analysis suggests that although social media may not always be the most effective channel for direct and active forms of participation and dialogue between citizens and government, it does not mean that social media can not be an effective mediator in other ways that promote democracy. As such, it is perhaps time we expand our study of social media-enabled participation in public administration to account for forms of participation that are happening, not bidirectionally, but through other more indirect channels. By doing so, we can begin to truly understand the magnitude of social media's impact on government, including its interactions with the public, its policies, and its decision-making processes.
